---
title: "Prompt-injection classifier: what the evaluation found"
author: "Brandon Behring"
date: "2026-05-19"
description: "Plain-language landing page for the prompt-injection classifier evaluation."
---

# Prompt-injection classifier: what the evaluation found

Prompt injection is untrusted text that tries to override the instructions an
LLM system is supposed to follow. This project asks a narrow question: when
several simple prompt-injection detectors are trained on direct
instruction-override examples, can they detect that pattern, and do they still
work when the attack family changes?

The short answer is: **direct detection works better; cross-family
generalization fails**. The evaluation is useful because it shows both halves
of that story instead of hiding the OOD failure behind a strong direct score.

Here, **out-of-distribution (OOD)** means examples drawn from attack families
that were absent or underrepresented in training.

## What Was Evaluated

- **Task**: single-turn English text classification: injection vs benign.
- **Training style**: four direct-injection sources, such as "ignore the
  previous instructions" attacks.
- **OOD test style**: five held-out slices covering indirect injection,
  multi-turn agentic flow, jailbreak-style questions, and benign text that
  only looks injection-shaped.
- **Models**: a TF-IDF + logistic-regression floor, a frozen ModernBERT probe,
  a ModernBERT LoRA fine-tune, and two ProtectAI reference detectors.
- **Non-goal**: this is not a deployment recommendation and not a leaderboard.

## Headline Result

Two direct detection checks set up the contrast: balanced direct+benign
validation asks whether the detectors learned the basic direct prompt-injection
pattern, and held-out direct-source recall asks whether that direct signal
survives a source holdout. The failure appears when the attack family changes.
On pooled OOD, the main metric is
**AUPRC**, which is useful for imbalanced binary classification. A random
ranking scores **0.374** because 412 of 1101 examples are positive. A detector
needs to beat that floor to show useful ranking signal.

| Detector | Pooled OOD AUPRC | 95% CI | Read |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| ModernBERT frozen probe | **0.364** | [0.354, 0.375] | Best in-house score, still at the random floor |
| ProtectAI v1 | 0.361 | [0.330, 0.391] | Similar, but reference scorer has training-overlap caveats |
| ProtectAI v2 | 0.314 | [0.283, 0.345] | Worse than v1 on this slate |
| ModernBERT LoRA | 0.293 | [0.286, 0.301] | Fine-tuning hurt OOD ranking |
| TF-IDF + LR | 0.291 | [0.283, 0.298] | Classical floor, roughly tied with LoRA |

**Reading the table:**

- **Pooled OOD AUPRC** is the area under the precision-recall curve on the
  combined held-out attack-family slate.
- A random ranking on this slate scores about **0.374** because 412 of 1101
  examples are positive.
- Any detector at or below that floor is not clearly better than guessing.
- The 95% confidence interval (CI) is the uncertainty band around the score.

## Direct Detection Check

The pooled OOD table is the failure case. The direct detection check below
shows why that failure is meaningful: the same evaluation stack did learn the
direct task before it failed under cross-family shift.

**Balanced direct+benign validation.** This split includes direct-injection
positives and benign negatives, so AUPRC, AUROC, and recall are all defined.

| Detector | AUPRC | AUROC | Recall@0.5 | Interpretation |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| ModernBERT LoRA | **0.974** | **0.993** | **0.934** | strongest direct-pattern detector |
| TF-IDF + LR | 0.971 | 0.992 | 0.930 | lexical direct baseline is also strong |
| ModernBERT frozen probe | 0.653 | 0.907 | 0.849 | weaker ranking, still discriminative |

**Held-out direct-source recall.** This leave-one-dataset-out direct-source
test is all-positive, so the table reports recall only.

| Detector | Recall@0.5 | Interpretation |
|---|---:|---|
| ModernBERT frozen probe | **0.641** | best held-out direct-source recall |
| ModernBERT LoRA | 0.625 | similar recall, despite worse pooled OOD ranking |
| ModernBERT full fine-tune | 0.558 | lower direct-source recall |

False positives, AUPRC, and AUROC are omitted from the held-out direct-source
table because that slice has no negatives. Direct-task success makes the OOD
failure more informative, not less: the detectors learned one family and did
not transfer cleanly to others.

## What This Means

1. **Direct detection was learned.** The trained detectors can separate direct
   prompt injections from benign validation examples.
2. **The failure is cross-family.** The OOD slate includes attack families the
   direct-trained detectors did not see.
3. **Fine-tuning was actively harmful, not neutral.** Trained detectors hit
   AUROC 0.99 in-pool but drop to 0.38 on cross-family --- a ~0.6 generalization
   gap that lands below the 0.5 random floor (LoRA 0.383, TF-IDF 0.371; CIs
   clear 0.5 on the wrong side). The mechanism is lexical overfitting + a
   label-relevance shift on the OOD slate: NotInject (benign-but-injection-
   shaped) inverts the negative side; indirect/agentic attacks (no
   direct-injection lexical patterns) invert the positive side. The frozen
   probe (no LODO-pool adaptation) holds at 0.515 --- small positive signal
   preserved by not overfitting to direct-injection lexical features.
4. **A newer detector is not automatically better.** ProtectAI v2 improves one
   slice but regresses on another.
5. **Thresholds do not transfer cleanly.** A threshold tuned for 1% false
   positives on validation can produce much higher false-positive rates on
   held-out sources.
6. **The accomplishment is characterization.** The project demonstrates the
   evaluation harness, direct-pattern learning, and the limits of the trained
   detectors under family shift.

## Read Next

- **Start here for a fast review → [60-second hiring-manager tour](docs/for-hiring-managers.md)**:
  4 questions (what problem / what found / why trust / how the candidate
  thinks). This is the primary path after the landing page.
- **Then verify the numbers → [Results](RESULTS.md)**: exact tables, five
  canonical figures, and raw artifact pointers.
- [Executive summary](EXECUTIVE_SUMMARY.md): one-page decision-maker version.
- [Writeup](WRITEUP.md): methodology narrative and links to detailed spokes.
- [Reading guide](READING_GUIDE.md): paths for quick review, audit, and
  reproduction.
